rulings.Case Title: JENNIFER GOSK VS. ACADEMY OF ART UNIVERSITY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Cross Defendant Century Campus Housing Management. L.P.'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2013, Line 13, Cross Defendant Century Campus Housing Management. L.P.'S Motion For Summary Judgment. CLV's motion for summary judgment is denied. CLV did not meet its burden of production of evidence to demonstrate that absence of a material issue of fact and that it is thus entitled to judgment as a matter of law. Items 6 and 7 at page 5 of the Cross-Defendants memorandum, and UMF 13, taken together are sufficient to obviate the moving parties' argument regarding the issue of material fact. AAU creates a triable issue of material fact regarding CLV's negligence. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether CLV was aware of the dangerous condition in the bathroom and failed to attempt to remedy it in a timely fashion. Plaintiff testified that the leaky sink was a perennial problem and the resident supervisors (Danny Hall and Jeannette Esteves) were aware of the problem prior to the incident. (P's depo, 107:1-24, 117:18-120:7, 120:18-121:13, 141:13-18, 141:19-142:14, 150:23-151:4). CLV's PMK (Dr. Doris Collins) testified that she did not know when inspections of the bathroom were made and nobody reported a leak a week prior to the accident. (Collins depo, 61:21 - 67:10; 81:3-23). rulings.Case Title: PROTECT OUR BENEFITS VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Protect Our Benefits' Petition For Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2013, Line 14, Petitioner Protect Our Benefits' Petition For Writ Of Mandate. Petitioner has not presented evidence compelling the conclusion that Subsection (d) is unreasonable and invalid. (City of San Diego v. Haas (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 472, 496.) Petitioner's argument that Subsection (d) is an impairment on contract because it essentially prohibits supplemental COLAs to retirees because supplemental COLAs have been issued since 1996 is flawed. Petitioner concedes that "retirees did not receive supplemental COLAs in 1996, 2003, 2008 and 2009." (Points & Auth., p. 4:4-5. See Req. Jud. Notice, Exhibit C.) Further, supplemental COLAs have historically been subject to conditions. (City Charter § A8.526-1(a) & (b).) Subsection (d) does not abrogate the spirit of these prior amendments. (United Firefighters of Los Angeles City v. City of Los Angeles (1989) 210 Cal.App.3d 1095, 1102-03.) Petitioner has not provided sufficient legal authority to support the proposition that the Court may question the accuracy of language contained in a voter-approved initiative and invalidate it on this basis. Further, " 'The voters are presumed to be aware of existing law [citation], including the administrative enforcement provisions. [Citations.]" (Sacks v. City of Oakland (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 1070, 1085.) Lastly, Petitioner has not presented sufficient evidence to show that the actuarial reports presented to the Board of Supervisors by Cheiron do not comply with the requirements of the City Charter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: GORDON & REES LLP VS. STEVEN ANTHONY LOPEZ et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Andmotion To Compel Verified Further Responses To Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 13, 2013, Line 1: Defendants ERIC KOEHLER'S and STEVEN LOPEZ'S Motion to Compel Verified Further Responses to Form Interrogatories Pro Tem Judge Mark Schreiber, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: GRANT IN PART AND DENY IN PART. THE MOTION IS GRANTED TO THE EXTENT THAT PLAINTIFF GORDON & REES LLP SHALL SERVE A SINGLE SET OF FURTHER RESPONSES TO FORM INTERROGATORIES, SET NO. 1, ON BEHALF OF ITSELF ONLY EXCEPT FOR ALL QUESTIONS IN SECTION 2 AND QUESTIONS 8.2 THROUGH 8.8 IN SECTION 8. THE MOTION IS DENIED TO THE EXTENT THAT IT CALLS FOR RESPONSES FROM ANY NON-PARTY, INCLUDING THE FOURTEEN INDIVIDUALS IDENTIFIED IN THE FOURTEEN SETS OF FORM INTERROGATORIES. NO SANCTIONS ARE AWARDED. THE FURTHER RESPONSES OF PLAINTIFF GORDON & REES LLP SHALL BE SERVED WITHIN FOURTEEN DAYS OF NOTICE OF THIS ORDER. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to MSchreiber@cwclaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT CARTER et al VS. PACIFIC UNION REAL ESTATE GROUP LTD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Brookfield Asset Management Inc. & Brps Llc'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, August 13, 2013, Line 15, Defendant Brookfield Asset Management Inc. & BRPS LLC'S Motion For Summary Judgment. Defendants' motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendants' motion for summary adjudication denied as to the first cause of action, and is granted as to the third, fourth and fifth causes of action. Defendants' request for judicial notice is granted. As to the first cause of action, a party may bring a wholly separate action to enforce a prior judgment against a corporation on an alter ego theory. (See Leek v. Cooper (2011) 194 Cal. App. 4th 399, 419, reh'g denied (May 12, 2011).) Defendant shifted the burden by producing evidence that Plaintiffs did not know, or were not aware of, any business controlling Defendant Pacific Union Real Estate Group, Ltd. ("PUREG"), what entity funded the previous litigation, the capitalization of PUREG, corporate governance, or any purchases agreements related to PUREG. (See Defendants' Separate of Undisputed Material Facts in Support of Their Motion for Summary Judgment Against Plaintiffs, Nos. 29-44; See also Declaration of Scheiderer in Support of Motion of Summary Judgment Against Plaintiff, Exh. J and K.) Consequently, the burden shifted to Plaintiffs to show a triable of material fact existed regarding alter ego. Plaintiffs produced sufficient evidence to show that a triable issue of material fact exists regarding whether Defendants have a unity of interest between Defendants and PUREG. (See Elliot v. Occidental Life Ins. Co. of Cal. (1969) 272 Cal. App. 2d 373, 377.) Plaintiffs have shown that PUREG's accounting, human resources, information technology, facilities, and legal departments were all managed or controlled by employees of Defendant Brookfield Asset Management Inc. (See Declaration of Losch in Support of Opposition to Summary Judgment ("Losch Declaration"), Exh. L. Tr. 71:6-22; 101:24-104:9.) Plaintiffs produce evidence that Defendants controlled the previous litigation. (See Losch Declaration, Exh. T, T. 59:15-61:1.) Plaintiffs also produce evidence that Defendants had the same officer and director as PUREG. (See Losch Declaration, Exh. R; Meyer Declaration in Support of Opposition to Opposition, Exh. D.) Plaintiffs' motion to continue is denied as moot. This is sufficient evidence to create a triable issue of material fact regarding the theory of alter ego. Defendants' motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the fourth cause of action. Defendants have produced evidence that PUREG was substantially in debt. (See Declaration of Ballot in Support of Summary Judgment, 7-10, Exh. M.) Plaintiffs have not produced any evidence that shows that PUREG had a distribution available to return to Plaintiffs. Defendant motion for summary adjudication as to the third and fifth causes of action is granted as moving Defendants are not named Defendants for those causes of actions in Plaintiffs Complaint.
